This  is  a  digital  copy  of  a  book  that  was  preserved  for  generations  on  library  shelves  before  it  was  carefully  scanned  by  Google  as  part  of  a  project 

to  make  the  world's  books  discoverable  online. 

It  has  survived  long  enough  for  the  copyright  to  expire  and  the  book  to  enter  the  public  domain.  A  public  domain  book  is  one  that  was  never  subject 

to  copyright  or  whose  legal  copyright  term  has  expired.  Whether  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  may  vary  country  to  country.  Public  domain  books 

are  our  gateways  to  the  past,  representing  a  wealth  of  history,  culture  and  knowledge  that's  often  difficult  to  discover. 

Marks,  notations  and  other  maiginalia  present  in  the  original  volume  will  appear  in  this  file  -  a  reminder  of  this  book's  long  journey  from  the 

publisher  to  a  library  and  finally  to  you. 

Usage  guidelines 

Google  is  proud  to  partner  with  libraries  to  digitize  public  domain  materials  and  make  them  widely  accessible.  Public  domain  books  belong  to  the 
public  and  we  are  merely  their  custodians.  Nevertheless,  this  work  is  expensive,  so  in  order  to  keep  providing  tliis  resource,  we  liave  taken  steps  to 
prevent  abuse  by  commercial  parties,  including  placing  technical  restrictions  on  automated  querying. 
We  also  ask  that  you: 

+  Make  non-commercial  use  of  the  files  We  designed  Google  Book  Search  for  use  by  individuals,  and  we  request  that  you  use  these  files  for 
personal,  non-commercial  purposes. 

+  Refrain  fivm  automated  querying  Do  not  send  automated  queries  of  any  sort  to  Google's  system:  If  you  are  conducting  research  on  machine 
translation,  optical  character  recognition  or  other  areas  where  access  to  a  large  amount  of  text  is  helpful,  please  contact  us.  We  encourage  the 
use  of  public  domain  materials  for  these  purposes  and  may  be  able  to  help. 

+  Maintain  attributionTht  GoogXt  "watermark"  you  see  on  each  file  is  essential  for  in  forming  people  about  this  project  and  helping  them  find 
additional  materials  through  Google  Book  Search.  Please  do  not  remove  it. 

+  Keep  it  legal  Whatever  your  use,  remember  that  you  are  responsible  for  ensuring  that  what  you  are  doing  is  legal.  Do  not  assume  that  just 
because  we  believe  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  the  United  States,  that  the  work  is  also  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  other 
countries.  Whether  a  book  is  still  in  copyright  varies  from  country  to  country,  and  we  can't  offer  guidance  on  whether  any  specific  use  of 
any  specific  book  is  allowed.  Please  do  not  assume  that  a  book's  appearance  in  Google  Book  Search  means  it  can  be  used  in  any  manner 
anywhere  in  the  world.  Copyright  infringement  liabili^  can  be  quite  severe.

INTRODUCTION. 


In  publishing  the  groat  South-EtiglM  Legeiulary  or  Festival  iu 
verse,^  one  of  the  most  important  works  of  meduBval  literature,  I 
begin  with  the  version  of  MS.  Laud  108.     Only  a  few  of  these 
legends  have  as  yet  been  published  separately,  mostly  from  MS. 
.  Ilarleian  2277 — long  regarded  as  the  oldest  MS. — ^by  H.  Black, 
Thos.  Wright,  Eorle,  Cockayne,  Morris,  and  some  by  Fumivall  in 
his  Eiirltf  Eu/jlisli  Poems  and  Lives  of  Saints  (with  those  of  the 
)  wicked  Birds,  Pilate  and  Judas),  Berlin,  1862.     The  Laud  MS.  I 
i  first  described  in  Lehen  Jesu,  ein  Fraffinent,  Miluster,  1873.    Of  the 
other  MSS.,  I  have  treated  at  large  in  Altonglisehe  Legenden^ 
Padorbora,  1875,  and  in  Altengliselie  Legenden^  Neue  Foige,  Heil- 
bronn,  1881,  pp.xliv — Ivii.    To  say  more  now,  to  make  out  the 
I  relation  of  all  the  MSS.,  the  history  of  the  text,  the  sources,  ^, 
\  the  time  has  not  yet  come  :  it  is  a  task  of  immense  difficulties,  on 
i  account  of  the  great  number  and  diversity  of  the  MSS.    I  hope 
^'  to  find  my  way  thi'ough  this  mass  of  materials,  and  to  lay  open 
the  relation  of  the  principal  MSS.,  in  the  Introduction  to  the  last 
volume;  but  it  will  require  more  brains,  the  brains  of  several 
generations  to  oome,  before  every  question  relative  to  this  collec- 
tion can  be  decided.    My  present  object  is  to  produce  the  MSS. 
themselves,  and  to  register  the  textual  tradition,  so  preparing  the 
way  for  further  investigation.    The  Collection,  in  its  complete 
state,  is  a  LilHir  Festivalis^  containing  sermons,  or  materials  for 
sermons,  for  the  Festivals  of  the  year  in  the  order  of  the  Calendar, 
and  comprehends  not  only  Saints'  lives  for  Saints'  Days,  but  also 
a  Temparale  for  the  festivals  of  Christ,  an  Advent  and  Christmas 
cycle,  the  Passion  and  ad  Easter  cycle,  and  even,  in  some  MSS.,  a 

^  Ths  litis  of  Um  eompkts  ooUsetion  wit  perhaps  ths  Uirrour  vf  SaitUs 
fAves,

of  Christ  compiled  from  the  Gospeb  and  Sunday  Lessons.  It 
made  shortly  before,  or  simultaneoualy  with,  the  Livfeuda  auma 
by  Jaoobos  a  Yoragine,  the  famous  store-book  of  medieval  legends, 
Keither  of  these  collections  is  the  source  of  the  other;  both  were 
formed  independently  of  one  another,  and  prove  that  the  same 
task,  which  was  indeed  required  by  that  titre,  was  attempted  by 
different  writers  at  different  places.  The  English  collection,  how- 
ever, was  not  completed  at  once,  or  by  one  p,3et.  Merely  to  collect 
the  materialsy^  the  Ft/cs,  which  liad  to  be  brought  together  from 
different  places,  as  no  library  of  the  present  type  then  existed, 
transcended  the  power  of  one  man.  The  Collection  gi^ew  slowly, 
and  expanded  by  degrees,  round  a  first  nucleus ;  it  was  tlie  work 
of  many  decades  of  years,  of  many  collaboratoi-s,  most  likely  the 
joint  work  of  a  whole  abbey,  that  of  Gloucester,  where  the  plan 
seems  to  have  been  fixed  and  brought  into  definite  shape.  Still  it 
is  possible  that  the  first  attempts,  the  oldest  pai-ts,  were  made 
before  and  at  other  places.  We  find  several  very  old  poems 
absorlted  into  the  Collection,  and  adapted  to  the  metre  (as  in  some 
MSS.  the  old  poem  on  the  Assumption,  old  texts  of  Magdalen, 
and  Margaret).  We  still  possess  some  fragments  of  Gospel  stories 
in  the  same  dialect  and  the  same  metra  from  the  beginning  of  the 
13th  century. — So  much  is  certain,  that  the  Collection  underwent 
more  than  one  phase  before  its  completion.  The  legends  do  not 
•eem  to  have  been  composed  at  first  in  the  order  of  the  Calendar, 
but  rather  as  the  materials  came  in  and  were  at  hand.  Before  the 
oomi^etion,  parts  of  the  Collection  seem  to. have  been  propagated 
by  MSS.,  and  to  have  been  augmented  by  new  additions  in  neigh- 
bouring abbeys,  which  thus  took  part  in  the  work.  In  tliis  way 
we  must  explain  why  the  existing  MSS.  form  nearly  as  muny 
different  redactions  or  versions.  MS.  HarL  2277  (c.  1300)  is  the 
first  MSL  which  gives  the  Collection  complete :  it  observes  strictly 
the  order  of  the  year,  and  contains  the  set  of  legends  which  remains 
stationary  in  the  later  MSS. ;  but  the  text  is  already  very  much 
oormpted,  and  must  have  gone  through  many  changes  before  it

irin,  and  appends,  at  the  end,  several  of  the  additions  of  MS. 
crton  (Ct.  Egwine,  Ailbrijt)  and  Harl.  (Theophle,  )»e  ri^te  put),

the  Saints ;  it  has  the  texts  peculiar  to  MS.  Laud  108),  MS.  Trin. 
ColL,  Gambr.  R  3,  25.    MS.  Bodley  779,  one  of  the  latest  MSS.,  is 
guite  a  new  version,  with  a  great  many  new-made  additions  (mostly 
popes'  lives).    The  Tempaml^  is  complete  in  none  of  these  MSS. ; 
they  either  contain  the  Passion  dec.,  or  the  Advent  and  Christmas

^cle,  or  ffFBgments  of  the  life  of  Christ ;  it  is,  however,  foancl

eontains  the  Temporale  (without  the  Saints'  lives),  and  is  the  only 
US,  which  has  the  whole  Life  of  Clurist  after  the  gospels.  None 
of  all  these  M8&  gives  the  original  text;  they  are  all  alike 
cofmpted,  which  proves  that  the  primitive  text  must  lie  far  back ; 
for  in  general,  a  MS.  will  be  clearer  and  more  correct  the  nearer 
it  is  to  the  original;  and  it  will  be  corrupted  in  proportion  to 
its  remoteness  from  the  first  text  The  original  text  will  have  to 
be  reconstructed,  with  difliculty,  from  the  older  MSS. 

MS.  Laud  108  stands  independent  from  all  other  MSS.  It  is 
the  oldest  of  all  existing  MSS.,  and  precedes  MSS.  HarL  and  Ashm. 
by  10 — ^20  years,  being  written  about  1 280 — 90 :  *  several  authorities 
I  have  consulted  agree  with  me  in  that  date.  It  shows  the  collec- 
tion still  in  an  unfinished  state,  in  the  way  of  progress,  before  its 
ecmip!etion ;  it  contains  only  two-thirds  of  the  items  in  MS.  Harl., 
and  these  not  strictly  in  the  order  of  the  year,  but  (it  seems)  in 
the  order  in  which  they  were  composed  and  ready-made.  It  ha£ 
some  peculiar  texts,  which  differ  from  MS.  Harl.,  and  seem  to  have 
belonged  to  the  very  first  redaction :  ss,  a  short  prologue,  combined 
with  3eres  day  and  St  Fabian ;  Conversion  of  St  Paul';  St  Bride 
(abort  text');  St  Silvestre;  a  different  text  of  St  Maudeleyn,  adapt- 
ed from  a  v^  old  poom  with  mid-rhymes^;  a  fragment  of  the  Life  oi 
Christ  after  the  Gospels^;  texts  which  were  adopted  by  some  latei 
MSSl  (Lambeth,Yemon)instead  of  the  Harleian  texts.  It  frequently 
preserves  the  original  reading,  where  all  the  other  MSS.  are  corrupt, 
and  win,  when  consulted  in  cases  of  difficulty,  help  us  to  find  the  inn 
Yet,  though  being  the  oldest  MS.,  it  is  far  from  being  correct, 
from  strictly  representing  the  original  text :  on  the  contrary,  the 
ibe  has  frequently  expanded  the  lines  by  added  words  and  phrases

^      and  altered  the  text  on  his  own  account,  where  the  original  meaning 

S'  was  perrerted  or  obscured.    These  alterations  are  not  alike  frequent 

)   in  all  parts ;  some  texts  are  very  nearly  correct    The  dialect  differs 

in  some  respects  from  that  in  which  the  original  was  composed,  and 

which  is  more  properly  represented  by  the  Harleian  and  Ajihmole 

•  MSS.;  certain  words  (f.  L  eke)  are  avoided ;  we  find  no  instances  of  v 

1  instead  of  /  (as  in  vur  =  fire),  of  u  instead  of  eo,  y  (as  dure  instead 

i  of  deore) ;  hi  (hy,  hii)  is  replaced  by  Auy  and  keo.    So  MS.  Laud 

I    is  not  only  the  oldest  MS.,  but  represents  a  separate  yersion, 

\    independent  from  the  generollyreceived  text.    For  these  reasons, 

I  it  was  deemed  advisable  to  have  the  Laud  MS.  printed  separately 

.   as  a  fintt  text,  to  be  followed  by  the  generally-received  text,  as 

represented  by  MS.  Harl.  and  the  other  old  MSS.    Moreover,  the 

Laud  various  readings  are  so  many,  that  they  would  have  swelled 

the  notes  to  a  single  later  text,  to  double  the  size. 

I  know  most  Englishmen  consider  it  not  worth  while  to  print 

all  these  Legends;  I  know  they  regard  them  as  worthless  stuff, 

without  any  merit,  because  they  are  wholly  absorbed  in  questions 

^  of  the  day,  of  politics  and  no  end,  in  the  fade  poetry  of  poets 

iureate  and  lady  authors,  which  to  an  intellect  of  the  middle 

^es  would  have  appeared  infinitely  more  insipid  (as  turning  on 

momentary  interests,  the  "self"  and  its  last)  than  these  L^nds 

/  may  appear  to  the  present  generation.  The  English  mind  is  always 

4    running  into  extremes  with  full  steam,  with  brutal  energy,  from 

i  Popery  to  *'  no  Popery,"  now  into  the  grossest  superstition,  and 
again  disclaiming  and  holding  in  abhorrence  what  their  own  fathers 
revered  and  held  in  awe ;  it  only  sees  its  present  objects,  and  is 
*  blind  to  everything  which  lies  behind  or  around ;  it  wants  the  JtuU 
^nilieu,  the  repose  of  a  contemplative  mind,  and  forgets  that  in 
^he  eyes  of  eternity  every  epoch,  every  faith,  has  its  raisoii  d'etre^ 
tuid  every  true  poetry  its  beauty.    If  the  present  English  public 

bot  see  any  merit  in  these  Legends,  it  does  not  follow  that 
)  is  no  such  merit.^    To  be  appreciated,  they  demand  an 
Intellect  more  robust  and  sane,  a  heart  more  wide  and  enlarged, 

^  Iios  Mgondet  dot  aaints,  poar  la  plupart,  na  tout  pu  hittoriqaet,  et 
n^unnoins  ellet  aont  menreilleaMmeiit  instmetiTet  poar  m  qui  tiout  &  la  oooleor 
don  tenipt  et  anz  bmbqis.— E.  Bciian,  Hist.  tPI$mei,  I.  rrrface* 


Zll  CnARACTSBISTfCS  OP  THB  UBOBHINI, 

a  mind  more  truly  Christian  and  less  hypocritical  than  the  pi'e- 
sent  generation  is  ahle  to  supply.  They  present  models  of  sane- 
tity,  models  of  self-ahnegation,  virginity,  meekness  and  obedience, 
virtues  which  are  not  to  the  taste  of  our  tima  But  if  the  present 
time  does  not  like  this  moral,  it  does  not  follow  that  the  moral 
is  bsd,  or  worse  than  our  own.  They  present  a  different  kind 
of  humanity :  suffering  humanity,  which  is  not  the  province  of 
**  our  conquering  heroes."  They  represent  the  Christian  mythology, 
as  it  had  been  formed  in  the  course  of  centuries.  Some  of  them 
are  historical  or  fixed  by  tradition,  others  are  the  result  of  fiction, 
typical  of  the  Christian  hero.  The  style  of  these  legends  is,  no 
donbt^  coarse  and  rude  to  the  modem  taste ;  but  it  is  popular, 
adapted  to  the  subject,  to  the  public,  and  to  the  occasion.  Tlie 
narrative  is  generally  happy  and  well  conducted.  Some  of  the 
stories  are  written  in  a  vigorous  style,  which  rises  to  dramatic 
force  (as  Thomas  Beket) ;  others  are  full  of  humour.  In  others, 
the  legend  is  the  vehicle  for  scientific  digressions  (as  in  St.  Kenelm, 
where  a  geographic  description  of  England  is  given ;  as  in  the  thii*d 
part  of  St  Michael,  which  treats  of  the  elements,  of  the  body  and 
soul).  Everywhere  we  find  dispersed  sallies  of  wit  and  sarcasm, 
which  spare  no  class,  no  sex,  not  the  clergy  itself.  So  the  Collec- 
tion deserves  attention,  not  only  from  an  hagiologic,  but  also  from 
a  poetic  and  literary  point  of  view.  In  publishing  it,  we  only  pay 
a  just  debt  to  the  past. 

Lastly,  I  have  to  thank  Dr.  Fumivall  for  the  kind  interest  he 
has  taken  in  this  publication,  and  for  the  help  he  gave  me,  in 
adding  most  of  the  head-linea  At  his  request,  Mr.  Wm.  Baynr^j 
wrote  the  Summary  of  the  Lives  prefixed  to  the  Text,  as  a  sub- 
atitate  for  the  Society's  usual  side-notes,  for  which  the  long  line? 
of  the  Legends  left  no  room.

10.  Tins  Holy  Rood.    Tho  Emperor  Conntantino  sent  his  mother  Holoua  1  - 
to  Jorusdlom  to  enquire  about  the  Holy  rood  (6).    \\rhen  she  caino 
to  Jerusalem,  a  Jew,  named  Judas,  told  her  that  his  grandfather 
Zachaeus  had  taken  part  in  burying  the  rood  on  the  hill  of  Cahrtury 
(24).    The  qnoon  then  announced  to  the  people  assembled  that  they 
must  bring  her  the  rood  on  penalty  of  being  burned.    But  they 
made  an  outcry,  declaring  that  Judas  iras  really  the  person  to  bo 
treated  with.    <fudas  was  seixed  by  the  queen's  orders,  and  the  rest 
were  allowed  to  go  (50).    On  refusing  to  give  information,  ho  was 
thrown  into  prison,  and  lay  there  soyou  days  without  meat  or  drink. 
On  the  seventh  day,  forced  by  hunger,  he  proceeded  to  tho  spot 
mentioned  by  his  ancestor  as  the  place  where  the  cross  lay.    AVhon 
they  itiachcd  this  place  they  took  spnde  and  shovel,  and  very  soon 
they  found  three  crosses — that  on  whirh  the  Saviour  was  put  to  death,  3 
and  those  of  the  two  thieves  (73,  70).    On  their  way  home,  they 
touched  the  dead  body  of  a  young  man  with  the  Holy  rood,  and  he 
came  to  life  (84).    The  devil  came  veiling  forth  from  the  body,  saying 
that  having  overcome  through  a  jTudas,  by  a  Judas  he  is  overcome. 
Judos  became  a  Christian,  and  took  the  name  of  Quiriao  (ltd).    Tho 
queen  made  a  shrine  of  silver  and  gold,  and  precious  stones,  and 
placed  the  rood  therein  (116).    On  tho  hill  of  (calvary  they  built  a 
noble  church  (117).    The  noils  of  the  cross  were  miraculously  found 
(124).    The  Holy  rood  was  found  on  the  3rd  of  Moy,  on  the  day 
call<xl  Holy-rood  day.    Through  a  tree  were  we  at  first  lost,  and 
through  a  tree  were  we  a^in  brought  to  life.    Adam  had  one  request  5 
after  he  was  driven  from  raradise,  that  in  due  time  the  Lord  might 
blot  out  his  sins  (14d).    He  begged  his  son  Soth  that  he  would  go  to 
Paradise,  and  implore  pardon  Sir  him  (167,  176).    Within  the  walls 
of  Paradise  Seth  saw  a  spot  with  beautiful  flowers  and  all  kinds  of 
fruit ;  near  this  was  a  well,  and  by  it  stood  the  tree  which  had  caused 
Adam  to  sin.    On  the  tree  he  saw  a  little  child :  that  child  was  the 
Son  of  God  (216).    An  angel  took  throe  kernels  from  the  tree,  and 
bade  Seth  place  them  under  his  fother*s  tongue,  and  notice  what 
sprang  from  these  after  he  was  buried  (217,  220).    Adam  was  buried 
in  the  valley  of  Hebron,  and  in  a  few  years  afterwards  three  small 
twigs  sprang  up.    Moses  found  the  three  trees  when  ho  led  the  people 
through  the  wilderness  (^0).    They  stood  there  till  the  time  of  David, 
who  carried  them  to  Jerusalem,  and  set  them  in  a  reserved  plaoo,  and  8 
built  a  wall  round  them  (252^.    When  he  visited  them  next  dav  they 
had  grown  into  ono  tree— typical  of  the  Trinity  (256).    King  Solomon 
felled  the  tree  in  order  to  use  it  at  the  building  of  tho  touple,  but  it

Near  tbo  church  of  St  Damian  ho  was  exhorted  in  a  Tision  to  prop  it

19.  Alb  AN.  St.  Alban  was  a  heathen  in  his  ^routh,  but  was  converted  67-

37.  Life  of  St.  Nicholas.    St  Nicholas  was  bom  of  high  parentage  240 
in  the  city  of  Patras  (2).    He  was  religious,  even  in  his  boynood,  and 
quick  at  learning  ^5, 16).    On  one  occasion  he  gave  money  to  a  family 

of  poor  girls  who  nad  been  forced  into  a  sinful  life  (33).  When  the 
Bisnop  of  Mirre  died  Nicholas  was  appointed  his  successor  (84).  In 
his  office  he  was  humble  and  helpful,  a  priest  to  all  men  (86).  The 
crew  of  a  ship  were  miraculously  saved  by  him  during  a  storm  (108). 
During  a  time  of  famine  ho  secured  sufiBcient  com  for  the  people 
through  a  miracle  (122).  Some  pilgrims  to  the  church  of  Mirre  had 
brought  ointment  with  thorn,  which  tlioy  had  received  from  the  devil 
in  the  shape  of  a  woman,  to  smear  to  ill  intent  on  the  holy  walls,  but 
the  saint  exposed  the  trick  (155,  189).  A  war  between  the  emperor  of  246 
Home  and  his  enemies  was  brought  to  an  end  through  St  li'icholas 
convincing  the  invaders  of  their  error  (211).  He  conciliated  three 
princes  and  their  foes,  and  rescued  the  princes  when  falsely  accused 
(232,  246).  Angels  appeared  to  him  bo/ore  his  death  (305).  Many 
miracles  are  recorded  of  him.  A  Christian  who  cheated  a  Jew  was 
crushed  to  death  by  a  cart  through  the  saint's  anger  (326,  356).  A 
man  who  had  failed  to  give  the  saint  a  cup  he  had  promised  lost  his 
son  by  drowning  (375) ;  but  the  father  having  repented,  St  Nicholas 
restored  the  boy  to  life  (424).  A  child  who  was  strangled  by  a  devil 
was  restored  to  life  by  the  saint  (508).

there  spranff  up  a  well  of  purest  water  (25).  He  was  reckoned  a  god 
by  the  people  (30).  He  died  towards  the  end  of  January  (33).

47.  Clement.  St.  Clement  was  bom  at  Home  (1).  His  kindred  were  322— 310

and  her  sons  sailed  was  wrecked,  and  when  she  was  saved  her  sons  "*

St.  Peter  of  the  sad  absence  of  his  mother  and  his  brothers  (146).

60.  John  the  Apostle.  St  John  the  Apostle  was  the  son  of  our  402—417 
liOixl's  aunt,  and  brother  of  St.  James  (1,2).  He  was  twelve  months 
younger  than  our  liord  (7).  St  John  was  brought  before  the 
cmpeix>r  Domitian's  judge,  condemned  on  account  of  his  faith,  and 
thrown  into  burning  oil,  but  came  out  unhurt  (45,  57).  Then  he 
was  outlawed  to  Patmos,  whera  he  wrote  the  Apocalynse  (72).  He  404 
returned  to  Ephesus,  and  there  restored  Drusiane  to  life  ([91).  At 
the  entreaty  of  the  Christians  of  Ephesus,  St  John  wrote  his  Gospel 
(131).  By  his  prayer  a  heathen  temple  fell  down  with  groat  noise 
(179).  In  order  to  convert  Bishop  Anstodemus,  he  drank  poison  and 
remained  unhurt  (197,  222).  Anstodemus  and  others  were  converted 
by  the  miraculous  powers  of  the  apostle  (241).  lie  turned  bows  and 
arrows  into  pure  gold,  and  stones  mto  gems  (263).  He  raised  a  dead 
man  to  life  (275).  At  his  bidding  the  restoxxxl  man  reproached  two 
deserting  Christians,  and  they  repented  (291).  He  justified  his  play* 
ing  with  a  pet  partridge,  as  the  mind,  like  a  bow,  must  deeds  unoend 
(325).  A  bishop,  who  was  entrusted  with  the  charge  of  a  younff  man, 
after  a  time  allowed  him  to  fall  into  sin  (359).  Having  houd  that  the 
vouth  had  become  an  outlaw,  St  John  roae  out  to  find  him  (415).  414 
lie  discovered  the  youth,  and  brouffht  him  back  to  virtue  (427).  In 
a  vision  of  our  Lord  and  His  disciples,  the  apostle  was  summoned  to 
heaven  (447).  He  ordered  a  four-cornered  pit  to  be  dug  dose  to  the 
altar  in  his  church ;  and  there  he  died  (454,  480).  St  Edwaid,  kine 
of  England,  gave  a  ring  to  the  apostle  in  the  guise  of  a  poor  man,  ana 
it  was  sent  back  to  him  with  a  message  (498,  513).
